title: Spain
quote: The sunniest people are sometimes carrying the weight that no one around them thinks to ask about.

body.content: There is a recurring tendency in fan works to reduce Spain to a single dimension: the eternally cheerful, unconditionally kind nation who smiles through everything. While that reading is not wrong, it is deeply incomplete. Spain is not a saint. He is selective, particular, and quietly petty — and the smile is a deliberate choice, not a reflection of his inner state.
He openly states that he hates conflict, and yet chooses to frame something as devastating as his own nation's civil war as a kind of festival. He knows. He is hurt. The composure is intentional.

What drives him is comfort, pleasure, and the specific things he genuinely loves — good food, good weather, the small rituals of a life well-lived. Anything that disrupts that ease is, to him, a genuine imposition. He is not endlessly accommodating; he simply picks his battles carefully.
He carries real grudges — most notably against England and France — and the animosity is genuine, even if it never spills outward. He controls it. But it is there.

His dynamic with Romano is also frequently misread. Spain's behaviour around Romano — the clinginess, the relentlessness, the refusal to let things go unaddressed — is specific to Romano alone. Romano wears everything on the surface: his frustrations, his discomfort, his emotions. There is very little concealment. Spain, by contrast, hides everything behind a composed exterior — smiling when he is tired, smiling when he is sick, smiling when he is close to breaking. Romano's transparency seems to matter to him in a way he cannot easily explain or replicate.

There is also something worth naming about how Spain functions in relation to others: he is drawn, instinctively, to people who seem to need something. A new nation, a difficult person, someone closed off — he moves toward them. He extends a hand. It reads more like a gut conviction that something needs to be done, and that he is the one to do it.

Paired with this is a provider's mentality that I find genuinely endearing. He wants to be useful to the people he cares about. He wants to feed them, take care of them, make sure no one around him goes without. There is a real warmth in that — and also, if you sit with it long enough, something quietly melancholy.
Because behind all of it — the generosity, the composure, the cheerfulness — there is something unresolved that Spain never puts into words. Something that the smile is working very hard to cover.

In that sense, he reminds me of Russia — another character whose outward expression tells only part of the story. The difference is that Spain's warmth is genuine. But genuine warmth and inner pain are not mutually exclusive. If anything, that combination is what makes him worth writing about.
